channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Japan’s Leader Invokes Her Hero, Shinzo Abe, to Drive Nationalist Agenda
item.description: Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi is leveraging her ties to Mr. Abe, the slain Japanese leader, as she pushes to reshape defense and the economy.
item.dc:creator: Javier C. Hernández
item.media:credit: Kentaro Takahashi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sanae Takaichi has frequently leveraged her ties to Shinzo Abe.

item.title: U.S. Military Presence in Jordan Makes It Iranian Target
item.description: Relying heavily on U.S. aid and protection, Jordan is unlikely to restrict the American military presence there, despite escalating Iranian airstrikes.
item.dc:creator: Raja Abdulrahim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: American and Jordanian soldiers participating in a military exercise northeast of Amman in 2022.

item.title: Israeli Banks Threaten to Cut Off Palestinian Economy
item.description: Two Israeli financial firms have long served as partners to Palestinian banks, handling transactions that make possible the West Bank’s imports and exports.
item.dc:creator: David M. Halbfinger and Adam Rasgon
item.media:credit: Simon Beni/Middle East Images, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The Bank Hapoalim tower in Tel Aviv. Bank Hapoalim and Israel Discount Bank have written to their Palestinian counterparts saying they will end banking relationships with them.

item.title: Dubai Firm Cuts Deal for New Port to Bypass Strait of Hormuz
item.description: A Dubai-based logistics giant unveiled a deal on Wednesday to develop shipping terminals on the United Arab Emirates’ east coast.
item.dc:creator: Pranav Baskar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tomas Munita for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ships in the Gulf of Oman east of the United Arab Emirates this week.

item.title: Why Are Young People in India Protesting?
item.description: A youth-led movement angry over the education system and a lack of jobs is posing a rare challenge to a government that has ruled for more than a decade with a firm hand.
item.dc:creator: Anupreeta Das
item.media:description: Activists and supporters of the Cockroach Janta Party clashed with police officers during a protest in New Delhi on Monday.

item.title: How the Trump Administration’s Nuclear Deal With Saudi Arabia May Work
item.description: The Trump administration announced an agreement with Saudi Arabia on a civilian nuclear program. Such deals, known as 123 Agreements, are designed to prevent the development of weapons.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel
item.media:description: President Trump with the Saudi crown prince, Mohammed bin Salman, in Washington last year.

item.title: Louvre Gallery Reopens After Brazen Jewel Heist
item.description: Thieves stole crown jewels from the Apollo Gallery at the Louvre Museum in October, forcing its closure. Tourists finally returned on Wednesday.
item.dc:creator: Catherine Porter
item.media:description: Visitors arrive during the reopening to the public of the Apollo Gallery, at the Louvre museum in Paris on Wednesday.

item.title: Canadian Wildfire Evacuees Outraged Over Lacking Support and Communication
item.description: Residents of northwestern Ontario say their warnings about fires they spotted were dismissed and official information as the flames spread often didn’t come at all.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai and Cole Burston
item.media:credit: Cole Burston for The New York Times
item.media:description: Swaths of burned forest from the Dryden 35 Wildfire, part of a series of wildfires that forced many to flee their homes in Northwestern Ontario this month.

item.title: Daniel Siad, Model Scout Who Connected Women to Epstein, Is Found Dead in France
item.description: Daniel Siad, who was found dead on Monday, repeatedly introduced Jeffrey Epstein to young women, according to messages he exchanged with Mr. Epstein. Mr. Siad had denied wrongdoing.
item.dc:creator: Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:description: A screenshot from a messaging app with a photo of Daniel Siad. The screenshot was included in the files released by the U.S. Department of Justice on Jeffrey Epstein.

item.title: U.S.-Saudi Nuclear Deal Stokes Israeli Concerns of Mideast Arms Race
item.description: An Israeli minister sought to downplay the agreement, while critics said it could spur other regional countries to pursue a nuclear program, and potentially the creation of a bomb.
item.dc:creator: Natan Odenheimer and Aaron Boxerman
item.media:description: Nir Barkat, Israel’s minister of economy, in Tel Aviv last year. He has been the most senior Israeli government official to comment so far on the expected U.S.-Saudi nuclear deal.

item.title: A Mexican Town Was Known for Lettuce. Now It’s Because of Cyclospora.
item.description: In central Mexico, a lettuce-growing region has been potentially linked to an outbreak in the U.S. that has sickened thousands.
item.dc:creator: Emiliano Rodríguez Mega and Fred Ramos
item.media:credit: Fred Ramos for The New York Times
item.media:description: Crop fields behind the Taylor Farms processing facility in Doctor Mora, Guanajuato, Mexico.

item.title: Ukraine’s New Military Chief Faces a Monumental Test
item.description: Maj. Gen. Mykhailo Drapatyi faces a balancing act between asserting the independence favored by military reformers and accommodating political input on battlefield strategy.
item.dc:creator: Marc Santora
item.media:credit: Ukrainian Presidential Press Service, via Reuters
item.media:description: In a photograph released by the Ukrainian Presidential Press Service, Maj. Gen. Mykhailo Drapatyi attended a meeting with President Volodymyr Zelensky in Kyiv, Ukraine, on Monday.

item.title: Japan Is Startled by Prime Minister’s Grueling Sleep Schedule
item.description: Sanae Takaichi has said she gets just “zero to three hours” of sleep a night, prompting a debate in the country over work-life balance.
item.dc:creator: Javier C. Hernández and Hisako Ueno
item.media:description: Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi at a meeting at Japan’s Parliament in Tokyo last week. A social media post by Ms. Takaichi about her schedule has prompted a debate about work hours and the hazards of sleep deprivation.

item.title: Coveted U.S. Nuclear Deal Is a Coup for Saudi Crown Prince
item.description: Saudi Arabia has for years sought a deal to obtain American nuclear technology. The agreement, signed on Wednesday, could deepen U.S.-Saudi ties for decades to come.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Nereim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Prince Mohammed bin Salman, the de facto Saudi ruler, and President Trump at the White House in November.

item.title: Debate Over Ancient Iranian Jewelry Worn by Zendaya to ‘Odyssey’ Event
item.description: The actress wore earrings to an event for “The Odyssey” that a London gallery has said were made from an ancient artifact.
item.dc:creator: Yola Mzizi
item.media:credit: Scott A Garfitt/Invision, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Zendaya at an event for “The Odyssey” in London this month.

item.title: Houthis Claim Strikes on 2 Saudi Oil Tankers in Red Sea
item.description: The Saudi authorities confirmed a strike on one of the ships. These are the first claimed attacks since the Iranian-backed Yemeni group announced a blockade on Saudi Arabia.
item.dc:creator: Pranav Baskar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A satellite image of the Bab al-Mandab Strait earlier this month.

item.title: What Visuals Show About Recent Damage to U.S. Military Sites in the Middle East
item.description: Video footage and satellite images reveal new details about Iranian strikes on U.S. targets during the latest flare-up in fighting.
item.dc:creator: Aric Toler and Christoph Koettl
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces

item.title: Three Airports Plan to Ditch T.S.A. Agents Amid Push for Private Security
item.description: The airports have opted in to the agency’s new security model, an agency spokesperson confirmed on Wednesday. A union official said the move could jeopardize public safety.
item.dc:creator: Ryley Ober
item.media:description: Tampa International is one of three U.S. airports opting in to a new program.

item.title: Saudi Arabia’s Nuclear Plans
item.description: The U.S. will help the Saudis develop a civilian nuclear program, with uncertain consequences for the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold

item.title: U.S. Troops, Seriously Wounded in Recent Strikes, Were Flown to Germany for Help
item.description: The seriously wounded service members are among the roughly 100 American troops that the Defense Department recently said were injured by Iranian attacks since early July.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Donald Trump, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and other U.S. officials attending the arrival of U.S. Army soldiers killed in recent Iranian strikes.

item.title: Hitler’s Birthplace in Austria Is Now a Police Station
item.description: The government repurposed the 17th-century building in hopes of preventing the site from becoming an attraction for Nazi sympathizers.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses

item.title: Rubio and Chinese Diplomat Make Plans for Trump and Xi’s Washington Summit
item.description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio spent much of a meeting with China’s foreign minister discussing the summit, but did not bring up President Trump’s claims of election interference by China.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong
item.media:description: President Donald Trump with Xi Jinping in Beijing in May.

item.title: Trump’s 50% Tariffs on Canada: What to Know, and What’s Next
item.description: The president said punishing 50 percent tariffs would begin by Aug. 19, a significant escalation of the trade war between Canada and the U.S. that he started over a year ago.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:description: President Trump and Mark Carney in the Oval Office last year.

item.title: Firing of Ukraine’s Top General Signals the End of an Era
item.description: Gen. Oleksandr Syrskyi, who commanded crucial battles, oversaw an expansion of drone warfare against Russia, but many Ukrainians want even more.
item.dc:creator: Carlotta Gall
item.media:description: Gen. Oleksandr Syrskyi in a trench position north of Kyiv, the capital of Ukraine, in 2022.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt, Shirin Hakim, Alan Yuhas and Pranav Baskar

item.title: See Which 52 Places to Go We’ve Already Visited From Warsaw to Bangkok
item.description: Now halfway through the year, we’ve made it to 11 of our picks, uncovering lesser-known spots where the American Revolution was fought, all-night parties in Poland and more.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Hunter McRae for The New York Times
item.media:description: Black Mingo Creek was the site of a 1780 Revolutionary War skirmish. The writer Anna Venarchik paddled it as part of her article on Charleston, S.C., and its role in the war.

item.title: After Decades of Caution, Germany Is Shaking Up How It Makes Weapons
item.description: As threats from Moscow rise and support from Washington dwindles, the German military is reinventing how it researches, funds and wields 21st-century arms.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze
item.media:description: Testing a drone-jammer at Erding Air Base, Germany, in May.

item.title: K-pop’s NewJeans Hints at Comeback After Monthslong Feud With Its Label
item.description: The girl group had tried to cut ties with Ador, a subsidiary of the behemoth Hybe, but a court squashed that effort.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young
item.media:description: The five original members of NewJeans at a fashion event at Dongdaemun Design Plaza in Seoul, in 2024.

item.title: Iran’s Neighbors Long for a Deal, Any Deal, to End the War
item.description: Business owners in Pakistan are getting impatient for a chance to finally trade goods and oil along their long shared border with Iran.
item.dc:creator: Elian Peltier and Asim Hafeez
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Asim Hafeez for The New York Times

item.title: With His Latest Threats, Trump Signals to Iran That He Is Willing to Escalate
item.description: President Trump is facing a critical moment in the war, which has failed to bring about the “unconditional surrender” he once demanded.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: “We’ll be hitting that area probably pretty soon,” President Trump said of Pickaxe Mountain on Tuesday. “And there’s not a thing they can do about it.”

item.title: Mykhailo Drapatyi: What to Know About Ukraine’s New Top General
item.description: Maj. Gen. Mykhailo Drapatyi, a decorated commander, is popular both inside and outside of his country.
item.dc:creator: Cassandra Vinograd
item.media:description: Maj. Gen. Mykhailo Drapatyi speaking to journalists in Ukraine last year.

item.title: A Wartime Rift in Ukraine
item.description: President Volodymyr Zelensky fired his top general after a feud between old-school officers and younger innovators spilled into the streets.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.media:credit: Brendan Hoffman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Volodymyr Zelensky and the former commander in chief, Oleksandr Syrskyi, in March.

item.title: Trump Threatened to Strike Pickaxe Mountain, a Suspected Iranian Nuclear Site. Here’s What to Know.
item.description: The fortified underground complex, in central Iran, is about one mile away from the Natanz uranium enrichment facility.
item.dc:creator: Anushka Patil
item.category: Institute for Science and International Security
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A satellite image provided by Vantor shows an entrance to tunnels at Pickaxe Mountain in Iran last month.

item.title: Zelensky Dismisses Ukraine’s Top General After Protests
item.description: The move followed the firing of a popular defense minister, who had clashed with the general over how to fight the war.
item.dc:creator: Marc Santora
item.media:credit: Brendan Hoffman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Volodymyr Zelensky and the Ukrainian commander in chief, Oleksandr Syrskyi, in Ukraine’s Donetsk region in March.

item.title: Water Plants Under Fire in the Gulf Are Crucial to Civilians
item.description: Several days of airstrikes have hit water and energy plants in Kuwait, a nation that relies on desalination for 90 percent of its drinking water.
item.dc:creator: Zane Irwin
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Airbus DS SPOT CNES, via Reuters
item.media:description: A satellite image showing smoke rising from the Subiya Power and Water Desalination Plant in Kuwait on Saturday.

item.title: Inside the Fight to Rid South Africa of Black Immigrants
item.description: As immigration surges in the country, activists are going to extreme lengths to expel foreign nationals, often taking the law into their own hands.
item.dc:creator: John Eligon, Zimasa Matiwane and Joao Silva

item.title: 3 Myths Fueling Anti-Immigrant Sentiment in South Africa
item.description: As the nation convulses with protests and violence, data and research suggest that some of the biggest arguments against foreigners don’t stand up against the facts.
item.dc:creator: John Eligon
item.media:description: Supporters of the March and March movement, one of the main anti-immigrant organizations in South Africa, demonstrating in Johannesburg, in April.

item.title: India’s Young Protesters Are Bloodied but Unbowed
item.description: A day after police forcibly broke up demonstrations, thousands returned to the protest site in New Delhi to keep pushing for action over the country’s failings in education.
item.dc:creator: Pragati K.B., Anupreeta Das and Alex Travelli
item.media:description: Injured protesters at the main protest site in New Delhi on Tuesday. They have vowed to fight on for demands that include the resignation of India’s education minister.

item.title: Netanyahu Ad Uses a Questionable Tactic Against Bereaved Rival
item.description: A.I.-generated images showing Gadi Eisenkot, the Israeli leader’s top challenger in upcoming elections, played on anti-Arab sentiments and used images seen as disrespectful to his son, who was killed in the recent Gaza war.
item.dc:creator: David M. Halbfinger
item.media:description: Gadi Eisenkot, leader of the Yashar party, speaking at the launch of his party’s election campaign in Drom HaSharon, Israel, last month.

item.title: France Joins a Global Move Toward Restricting Social Media for Children
item.description: Its new law, the first of its kind enacted by a European country, will ban access to some accounts for anyone under 15.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler

item.title: Iran’s top diplomat says ‘security gap’ may have led to strikes on supreme leader.
item.dc:creator: Yeganeh Torbati
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Abbas Araghchi, Iran’s foreign minister, during a news conference in New Delhi in May.

item.title: British Politician Ann Widdecombe Was Murdered with a Hammer, Prosecutors Say
item.description: The politician, Ann Widdecombe, was eating lunch at home when a man walked in and asked whether she had bank cards, security footage showed.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:description: The British former Prime Minister Liz Truss leads a tribute to the slain politician Ann Widdecombe in London last week.

item.title: 1,700 H.I.V. Treatment Sites Closed After Trump Aid Cuts, a Study Finds
item.description: New research also shows that children and high-risk adult populations have been particularly affected.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.category: amfAR, The Foundation for AIDS Research
President's Emergency Plan for AIDS Relief
item.media:description: A health clinic in Johannesburg that was closed in 2025, following cuts in U.S. aid.

item.title: Trump Escalates Canada Tariffs as Mark Carney Holds Firm
item.description: The president’s new tariffs on a top U.S. trading partner aim at gaining concessions, but the Canadian government, and the public, have been bracing for the escalation.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.media:description: Steel coils at the Algoma Steel facility in Ontario in July. U.S. tariffs on goods such as steel, aluminum and vehicles have hit sensitive Canadian sectors.

item.title: Lebanese Army Accuses Israeli Troops of Firing as Its Forces Deploy in ‘Pilot Zone’
item.description: The action raised tensions as the sides were taking their first steps under a U.S.-brokered deal to transfer control of occupied areas to Lebanon after months of war.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Lebanese army troops in Zawtar al-Gharbiya, southern Lebanon, after Israeli forces withdrew from the area on Tuesday.

item.title: Philippines Says Sailor Was Injured in Skirmish With Chinese Coast Guard
item.description: China said it was not the aggressor in the episode, which was the latest flare-up in the South China Sea between the two countries.
item.dc:creator: Max Kim
item.media:description: Philippine Navy personnel and China Coast Guard personnel clashing near a grounded Philippine warship at Second Thomas Shoal, in the South China Sea, on Monday.

item.title: A Family’s Daring Escape From North Korea
item.description: Their resolve for freedom hardened during the pandemic, when they lived through a crippling food shortage and were forced to witness frequent public executions.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun
item.media:credit: Woohae Cho for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kim Yu-mi, right, defected with a group that included her two children and her mother. Life in South Korea has not been quite what they imagined, but they harbor no regrets from deserting the North’s brutal dictatorship and relentless propaganda.

item.title: New UK Prime Minister Burnham Promises Hope and Change. The Hurdles Are High.
item.description: Britain’s new prime minister, Andy Burnham, promised policies that would give average Britons financial “breathing space.” His opponents are already doling out criticism.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:description: Prime Minister Andy Burnham speaking in front of 10 Downing Street on Monday. “We will make this moment a circuit breaker for Britain,” he said.

item.title: Flash Floods Kill at Least 23 in Afghanistan
item.description: Dozens remained missing after floods swept through a city in Nuristan, an isolated province in the country’s east.
item.dc:creator: Elian Peltier and Safiullah Padshah
item.media:description: A damaged bridge and building in the eastern province of Nuristan in Afghanistan on Tuesday. Heavy monsoon rains sent torrents of water down surrounding mountains.

item.title: When Protesters Speak, Zelensky Listens, in the Ukrainian Tradition
item.description: With anger high over the firing of the defense minister, the president’s office is signaling an openness to compromise.
item.dc:creator: Andrew E. Kramer
item.media:description: A photograph released by the Ukrainian Presidential Press Service on Monday showing President Volodymyr Zelensky meeting with Gen. Mykhailo Drapaty, the commander of the Joint Forces, in Kyiv.

item.title: The Teenage Boys In Search of Their Mother After the Venezuela Earthquake
item.description: After Venezuela’s devastating earthquakes, Winder and Deivi Delfín dug every day for 26 days to find the bodies of their mother, brother and grandmother.
item.dc:creator: Julie Turkewitz and Adriana Loureiro Fernandez
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Winder Delfín, 17, right, and his brother Deivi, 15, during the 24th day of searching for their mother, younger brother and grandmother, who lived on the first floor of a twelve story building that collapsed during the twin earthquakes last month.

item.title: Houthis in Yemen Edge Closer to Entering U.S.-Iran War
item.description: The Iran-backed Houthi militia in Yemen has declared a Red Sea blockade on Saudi Arabia, potentially opening a new front in the war rippling across the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Ismaeel Naar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Yemeni supporters of the Iran-backed Houthi movement gather during a rally against what the group says are restrictions imposed by the Saudi-led coalition on Houthi-controlled areas of Yemen, in Sana last week.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak, Jenny Gross, John Ismay and Catie Edmondson

item.title: Burnham, UK’s New Prime Minister, Starts With Electricity Tax Cut
item.description: Prime Minister Andy Burnham moved quickly to confront the high cost of living. And President Trump wished him well after a phone call between the two leaders.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:description: Prime Minister Andy Burnham of Britain outside No. 10 Downing Street on Monday. In a statement, Mr. Burnham said that he wanted “to give people breathing space.”
